entries.text: You can perfectly understand Native American resistance to Euro-colonialism. You can perfectly well understand it without any anti-Europeanism, anti-whiteism, anti-Christianism. They didn't want to cede their country to invaders. That's completely understandable.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You're minimizing the antisemitic element in Arab nationalism.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You minimized it. In all your books, you minimized it.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, no, no. Husseini was an antisemite. The leader of the Palestinian national movement in the '30s and '40s was an antisemite. This was one of the things which drove him, and also drove him in the end to work in Berlin for Hitler for four years, giving Nazi propaganda to the Arab world, calling on the Arabs to murder the Jews. That's what he did in World War II. That's the leader of the Palestinian Arab National Movement. And he wasn't alone. He wasn't alone.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Professor Morris, if you read your book, Righteous Victims, you can read it and read it and read it and read it, as I have, you will find barely a word about the Arabs being motivated by antisemitism.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: It exists, though.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I didn't say it doesn't exist.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Ah, you agree that it exists?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Hey, I don't know a single non-Jew who doesn't harbor antisemitic sentiments.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: We're talking about Arabs now.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Yeah, but I don't know anybody. That's just part of the human condition.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Antisemitism?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: And among the Arabs?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: So Professor Morris, here's my problem. I didn't see that in your Righteous Victims. Even when you talked about the first Intifada, and you talked about the second Intifada, and you talked about how there was a lot of influence by Hamas, the Islamic movement, you even stated that there was a lot of antisemitism in those movements, but then you went on to say, “But of course, at bottom, it was about the occupation. It wasn't about” And I've read it.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Yeah, but you're moving from different ages, across the ages.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: No, I'm talking about your whole book.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: The occupation began in '67, the one you're talking about.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I looked and looked and looked for evidence of this antisemitism as being a chief motor of Arab resistance to Zionism. I didn't see it.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Did he make that claim?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I don't remember the word chief. It's one of the elements.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Yeah. It's very binary thinking when it comes to-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Binary?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Yes, binary.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Please, don't give me this postmodernism binary'. You're the one that said the chief motor-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: But you are thinking in terms of black and white. Steven has a point.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Do you have your book here? Page 137.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You're talking in black and white concepts when history is much grayer. Lots of things happen because of lots of reasons, not one or the other, and you don't seem to see that.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Can I ask you a question? Because it's for them to talk too. Can I ask you a very quick question? What do you think the ideal solution was on the Arab side from '47? What would they have preferred? And what would've happened-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, they were explicit.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: And then the second one, what would've happened if Jews would've lost the war in '48? What do you think would've happened to the Israeli population, the Jewish population?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I think the Palestinians and the Arabs were explicit that they wanted a unitary, I think, federal state, and they made their submissions to [inaudible 01:28:09], they made their appeals at the UN General Assembly.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: What do you mean by unitary and federal? I don't get that. They wanted an Arab state. They wanted Palestine to be an Arab state.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yes. Yes.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Put it simply. That word, unitary, federal, they wanted Palestine as an Arab and exclusively Arab state. That's what they wanted.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: No, no, it wasn't an exclusively Arab state. I think we have to distinguish between Palestinian and Arab opposition to a Jewish state in Palestine on the one hand, and Palestinian and Arab attitudes to Jewish existence in Palestine, and there's a fundamental difference-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Well, Husseini, the leader of the movement, said that all the Jews who had come since 1917, and that's the majority of the Jews in Palestine in 1947, shouldn't be there.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, he did say-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: They shouldn't be citizens and they shouldn't be there.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: [inaudible 01:28:59]. The PLO charter said that in '64. I'm not going to deny it. Of course, it's true. I can understand the sentiment, but I think it's wrong.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Also, [inaudible 01:29:11] because you had the used the words earlier, that it was supremacy and exclusivity that the Zionist state-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, I want to answer your question. Husseini did say that, and I'm sure there was a very substantial body of Palestinian Arab public opinion that endorsed that. But by the same token, I think a unitary Arab state, as you call it, or a Palestinian state, could have been established, with arrangements, with guarantees to ensure the security and rights of both communities. How that would work in detail had been discussed and proposed, but never resolved. And again, I think Jewish fears about what would've happened-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: A second Holocaust.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, no-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: That was the Jewish fear. A second Holocaust.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, that may well have been the Jewish fear. It was an unfounded Jewish fear.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: It was unfounded?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Of course it was unfounded.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: What about like in '48 and '56 and-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: You really think that the Palestinians, had they won the war, were going to import ovens and crematoria from Germany and-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I don't know about that, but in almost every single Arab state where there were Jews living, after '48, after '56, after '67, there were always pogroms, there were always flights from Jews from those countries to Israel afterwards. I don't think it would be-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I wouldn't say there were always pogroms in every Arab state. I think there was flight of Arab Jews for multiple reasons, in some cases for precisely the reasons you say. If you look at the Jewish community in Algeria, for example, their flight had virtually nothing to do with the Arab-Israeli conflict. The issue of Algerian Jews was that the French gave them citizenship during their colonial rule of Algeria, and they increasingly became identified with French rule, when Algeria became independent and all the French ended up leaving, out of fear, or out of disappointment, or out of whatever, the Jews were identified as French rather than Algerian.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: This is a bit of a red herring. There were pogroms in the Arab countries. In Bahrain even, where there's almost no Jews, there was a pogrom in 1947. There was a pogrom in Aleppo in 1947.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I'm not denying any of that history.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: There were killings of Jews in Iraq and Egypt in 1948/49. But the Jews basically fled the Arab states, not for multiple reasons. They fled because they felt that the governments there and the societies amid which they had lived for hundreds of years no longer wanted them.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Look, without getting into the details, I think we can both agree that ultimately a clear majority of Arab Jews who believed that after having lived in these countries for centuries-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Way before the Arabs arrived there. Way before the Arabs arrived in Iraq.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: for centuries, if not millennia, came to the unfortunate conclusion that their situation had become untenable. I also think that we can both agree that this had never been an issue prior to Zionism and the emergence of the state of Israel.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: This isn't true. There were pogroms prior to Zionism.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Look, I'm not-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Pogroms didn't begin with Zionism in the Arab world.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: The issue is the point I raised, which is whether these communities had ever come to a collective conclusion that their position had become untenable in this part of the world. No, they were Arab Jews.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Well, because untenable meant there was no alternative. But with the creation of Israel, there was an alternative, right? A place where they could go and not be discriminated against or live as second class citizens or be subject to Arab majority states.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I also think it's interesting that when you analyze the flight of Jewish people, and I've seen this, I agree with you, it wasn't just a mass expulsion from all the Arab states. There were definitely push factors. There were also pull factors. Now, I don't know how you guys feel about the Nakba, but when the analysis of the Nakba comes in, again, it's back to that; well, that was actually just a top-down expulsion. The retreat of wealthy Arab people in the '30s didn't matter. Any of the messaging from the surrounding Arab states didn't matter. It was just an expulsion from Jewish people or people running from their lives from Jewish massacres. Again, I feel like it's a selective critical analysis of the-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Again, I'm a little uncomfortable always using the term Jewish here, because it wasn't the Jews of England or the Soviet Jews.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Well, I say Jewish because prior to '48, saying Israeli-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I think it's useful to refer to Zionists before 1948 and Israelis after '48. We don't need to implicate Jews of all-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Well, sure, but the Jewish people that were being attacked in Arab states weren't Zionists. They were just Jews living there, right?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Okay, I need to just comment on that. I was rereading Shlomo Ben-Ami's last book, and he does at the end discuss at some length the whole issue of the refugee question bearing on the so-called peace process. And on the question of '48 and the Arab immigration, if you'll allow me, let me just quote him. “Israel is particularly fond of the awkwardly false symmetry she makes between the Palestinian refugee crisis and the forced immigration of 600,000 Jews from Arab countries following the creation of the state of Israel, as if it were an unplanned exchange of populations.'.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: And then Mr Ben-Ami, for those of you who are listening, he was Israel's former foreign minister, and he's an influential historian in his own right, he says, “In fact, envoys from the Mossad and the Jewish Agency worked underground in Arab countries and Iran to encourage Jews to go to Israel. More importantly, for many Jews in Arab states, the very possibility of immigrating to Israel was the combination of millennial aspirations. It represented the consummation of a dream to take part in Israel's resurgence as a nation.”

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: So this idea that they were all expelled after 1948, that's one area, Professor Morris, I defer to expertise. That's one of my credos in life. I don't know the Israeli literature. But as it's been translated in English, there's very little solid scholarship on what happened in 1948 in the Arab countries which caused the Jews to leave.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Arab Jews.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Arab Jews, right. But Shlomo Ben-Ami knows the literature. He knows the scholarship. He's a historian.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: He comes from Tangiers.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: He's from Morocco. [inaudible 01:36:10] from Iraq has written on this issue as well.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: And the Jews in the Arab lands were not pro-Zionists. They weren't Zionists at all. Certainly [inaudible 01:36:18] family was anti-Zionist.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: And [inaudible 01:36:21], when he was interviewed by Marin Rappaport, on this question he said, “You simply cannot say that the Iraqi Jews were expelled. It's just not true.” And he was speaking as an Iraqi Jew who left with his family in 1948.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: They were pushed out. They weren't expelled. That's probably the right phrase. They were pushed out.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, I think it's more complex than that. Sorry, I interrupted you.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: No, you're not interrupting me, because I only know what's been translated into English, and the English literature on the subject is very small and not scholarly. Now, there may be a Hebrew literature, I don't know, but I was surprised that even Shlomo Ben-Ami, stalwart of his state, fair enough, on this particular point he called it false symmetry.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, no, Steven is right. There was a pull and a push mechanism in the departure of the Jews from the Arab lands post '48. But there was also a lot of push. A lot of push.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: That's indisputable. There was push-

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman
entries.text: And on the point of agreement, on this one brief light of agreement, let us wrap up with this topic of history and move on to modern day. But before that, I'm wondering if we could just say a couple of last words on this topic. Steven?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Yeah. I think that when you look at the behaviors of both parties in the time period around '48, or especially '48 and earlier, there's this assumption that there was this huge built-in mechanism of Zionism, and that it was going to be inevitable from the inception of the first Zionist thought that appeared in Herzl's mind that there would be a mass violent population transfer of Arab Palestinians out of what would become the Israeli state. I understand that there are some quotes that we can find that maybe seem to possibly support an idea that looks close to that, but I think when you actually consult the record of what happened, when you look at the massive populations that Israel was willing to accept within what would become their state borders, their nation borders, I just don't think that the historical record agrees with the idea that Zionists would've just never been okay living alongside Arab Palestinians.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: But when you look at the other side, Arabs would out of hand reject literally any deal that apportioned any amount of that land for any state relating to Jewish people or the Israeli people. I think it was said, even on the other end of the table, that Arab Palestinians or Arabs would've never accepted any Jewish state whatsoever.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: So it's interesting that on the ideology part where it's claimed that Zionists are people of exclusion and supremacy and expulsion, we can find that in diary entries, but we can find that expressed in very real terms on the Arab side, I think in all of their behavior around '48 and earlier, where the goal was the destruction of the Israeli state, it would've been the dispossession of many Jewish people. It probably would've been the expulsion of a lot of them back to Europe. And I think that very clearly plays out in the difference between the actions of the Arabs versus some diary entries of some Jewish leaders.

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman
entries.text: Benny?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Well, one thing which stood out, and I think Mouin made this point, is that the Arabs had nothing to do with the Holocaust, but then the world community forced the Arabs to pay the price for the Holocaust. That's the traditional Arab argument. This is slightly distorting the reality. The Arabs in the 1930s did their utmost to prevent Jewish emigration from Europe and reaching Palestine, which was the only safe haven available, because America, Britain, France, nobody wanted Jews anywhere, and they were being persecuted in Central Europe and eventually would be massacred in large numbers. So the Arab effort to pressure the British to prevent Jews reaching Palestine's safe shores contributed indirectly to the slaughter of many Jews in Europe because they couldn't get to anywhere, and they couldn't get to Palestine because the Arabs were busy attacking Jews in Palestine and attacking the British to make sure they didn't allow Jews to reach this safe haven. That's important.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: The second thing is, of course, there's no point in belittling the fact that the Palestinian Arab National Movement's leader, Husseini, worked for the Nazis in the 1940s. He got a salary from the German foreign ministry, he raised troops among Muslims in Bosnia for the SS, and he broadcast to the Arab world calling for the murder of the Jews in the Middle East. This is what he did. And the Arabs since then have been trying to whitewash Husseini's role. I'm not saying he was the instigator of the Holocaust, but he helped the Germans along in doing what they were doing and supported them in doing that. entries.text: Oh, really? The United States wasn't a potential safe haven? The only one was Palestine.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: At the time.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: The United States had no room from the Atlantic to the Pacific for Jews?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: It did have room, but it didn't want Jews.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: So, that wasn't the only safe haven.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Shouldn't you be focusing your anger and outrage-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: America should be blamed for not letting Jews in during the '30s and '40s.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: They are blamed, but nobody blames them for the Holocaust.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Well, indirectly-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I've never heard it said that Franklin Delano Roosevelt was indirectly responsible for the Holocaust. I never heard that. Now, maybe it's in Israeli literature because the Israelis have gone mad. Yes, your prime minister said the whole idea of the gas chambers came from the mufti of Jerusalem.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: That's nonsense. We all know that's nonsense.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: But we also know that Netanyahu said it.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Netanyahu says so many things, which are absurd [inaudible 02:06:36]-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: And he happens to be the prime minister [inaudible 02:06:38]-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: [inaudible 02:06:38] serving prime minister of Israel.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I can't be responsible-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: You're not responsible for them, but it is relevant that he's the longest serving prime minister of Israel.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Unfortunately, it says something about the Israeli public, I agree.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yes, and he gets elected, not despite saying such things, but because he says such.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: His voters don't care about Hajj Amin al-Husseini or Hitler, they know nothing about

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: They will be [inaudible 02:07:00].

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: His base know nothing about anything, and he can say what he likes and they'll say yes, or they don't care if he says these things.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: You may well be right, but anyway, not to beat a dead horse, but I still don't understand-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Let's not beat a dead horse, you're right.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I'll just conclude by saying I don't understand why the Mufti of Jerusalem is relevant-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: He is relevant, but-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: The head of the national-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yitzhak Shamir is not relevant?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Shamir wasn't the head of the national movement. He represented 100 or 200 or 300 gunmen who were considered terrorists by the Zionist movement at the time. The fact that 30 years later he becomes prime minister, that's the crux of history.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: And his history is not-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Hajj Amin al-Husseini was the head of the Palestine Arab National Movement at the time.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Anyway-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: What can you do?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I think we're speaking past each other and I'll leave it there.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: We're not, I'm talking facts.

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman
entries.text: Let's move to the modern day and we'll return to history, maybe '67 and other important moments, but let's look to today, in the recent months, October 7th. Let me ask sort of a pointed question. Was October 7th attacks by Hamas on Israel genocidal? Was it an act of ethnic cleansing? Just so we lay out the moral calculus that we are engaged in, maybe-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: The problem with October 7th is this, the Hamas fighters who invaded Southern Israel were sent, ordered to murder, rape, and do all the nasty things that they did, and they killed some 1,200 Israelis that day and abducted them as we know, something like 250, mostly civilians, also some soldiers, took them back to dungeons in Gaza, but they were motivated not just by the words of their current leader in the Gaza Strip, but by their ideology, which is embedded in their charter from 1988, if I remember correctly, and that charter is genocidal. It says that the Jews must be eradicated basically from the land of Israel, from Palestine. The Jews are described there as sons of apes and pigs. The Jews are a base people, killers of prophets, and they should not exist in Palestine. It doesn't say that they necessarily should be murdered all around the world, the Hamas charter, but certainly the Jews should be eliminated from Palestine.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: And this is the driving ideology behind the massacre of the Jews on October 7th, which brought down on the Gaza Strip, and I think with the intention by the Hamas of the Israeli counter offensive because they knew that that counter offensive would result in many Palestinian dead because the Hamas fighters and their weaponry and so on were embedded in the population in Gaza, and they hoped to benefit from this in the eyes of world public opinion as Israel chased these Hamas people and their ammunition dumps and so on and killed lots of Palestinian civilians in the process. All of this was understood by Sinwar, by the head of the Hamas, and he strived for that, but initially he wanted to kill as many Jews as he could in the border areas around the Gaza Strip.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I'll respond directly to the points you made, and then I'll leave it to Norm to bring in the historical context. That Hamas charter is from the '90s, I think?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: 1988, so it's from the '80s. I think your characterization of that charter as antisemitic is indisputable. I think your characterization of that charter as genocidal is off the mark.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: It's implicit.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: And more importantly, that charter has been superseded by a new charter. In fact, it has been Well, there is-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: There is no new charter. There is an explanation, a statement [inaudible 02:11:06]-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: 2018, a political statement.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: 2000 and something.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: 2018, supposedly clarifying things which are in the charter, but it doesn't actually step back from what the charter says, eliminate Israel, eliminate the Jews from the land of Israel.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: In 2018, the Hamas charter, if we look at the current version of the charter-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: It's not called a charter. You're calling it a charter. It wasn't. The only thing called the charter is what was issued in 1988 by Yassin himself.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Anyway, it makes a clear distinction between Jews and Zionists in 2018. Now, you can choose to dismiss it, believe it, it's sincere, it's insincere, whatever-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Insincere is probably the right word.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Secondly, I'm really unfamiliar with fighters who consult these kinds of documents before they go on-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: They're brought up on this in their education system. In the kindergarten, they're told, “Kill the Jews.” They practice with make-believe guns and uniforms when they're five years old in the kindergartens of the Hamas-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: At the instruction of the commissioner-general of UNRWA, right?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I didn't say that. I said the Hamas has kindergartens and summer camps in which they trained to kill Jews, children aged five and six.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Secondly, you keep saying Jews, to which I would respond-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: They use the word Jews.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: To which I would respond that Hamas does not have a record of deliberately targeting Jews who are not Israelis. And in fact, it also doesn't have a record of deliberately targeting either Jews or Israelis outside Israel and Palestine, so all this talk of-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Unlike the Hezbollah, which has started targeting Jews outside of Palestine.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: We're talking about October 7th and Hamas. If you'd also like to speak about Hezbollah, let's get to that separately if you don't mind. So again, genocidal, well, if that term is going to be discussed, my first response would be let's talk about potentially genocidal actions against Israelis rather than against Jews for the reasons that I just mentioned. And again, I find this constant conflation of Jews, Israel, Zionism, to be a bit disturbing.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Secondly, I think there are quite a few indications in the factual record that raise serious questions about the accusations of the genocidal intent and genocidal practice of what happened on October 7th. And my final point would be, I don't think I should take your word for it, I don't think you should take my word for it. I think what we need here is a proper independent international investigation, and the reason we need that-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Of what?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Of genocide during this conflict, whether by Palestinians on October 7th or Israel thereafter, and the reason that we need such an investigation is because Hamas is There won't be any hearings on what Hamas did on October 7th at the International Court of Justice because the International Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide deals only with states and not with movements. I think the international criminal court, and specifically its current prosecutor, Karim Khan lacks any and all credibility. He's been an absolute failure at his job. He's just been sitting his backside for years on this file. And I think I would point out that Hamas has called for independent investigations of all these allegations. Israel has categorically rejected any international investigation, of course, fully supported by the United States. And I think what is required is to have credible investigations of these things because I don't think you're going to convince me, I don't think I'm going to convince you, and this is two people sitting across the table from each other.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, there's certain things you don't even have to investigate. You know how many citizens, civilians died in the October 7th assault-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yes, but that's not-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You know that there are lots of allegations of rape. I don't know how persuaded you are of those. They did find bodies without heads, which is-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: There were no beheadings of infants.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: There were some beheadings, apparently.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: The Israelis didn't even claim that in the document they submitted before the ICJ. Go read what your government submitted. It never mentioned beheadings.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Well, as far as I know, there were some people who were beheaded, but-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: We could bring it up right now.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You also deny that there were rapes there.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I didn't deny. I said I've not seen convincing evidence that confirms it. I've said that from day one, and I'll say it today, four and a half months later.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Do you know that they killed eight or 900 civilians in their assault?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: 900 civilians in the assault-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Absolutely, that seems to me indisputable.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Absolutely. That seems to me indisputable.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Oh, okay. Well, I'm glad that you're considering something-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I've said that from day one.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Well, to be clear, you haven't. You did a debate I don't remember the talk show, but you seemed to imply that there was a lot of crossfire and then it might've been the IDF that had killed a lot of-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I said that there is no question because the names were published in Haaretz. There is no question that roughly of the 1200 people killed, 800 of them were civilians-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: 850, fine. So I never said that, but then I said, “No, we don't know exactly how they were killed.” But 800 civilians killed, no, 850, no question there. And I also said on repeated occasions, there cannot be any doubt, in my opinion as of now with the available evidence, that Hamas was responsible for significant atrocities, and I made sure to include the plural.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: There's a lot of tricky language being employed here. Do you think of the 850-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: There's nothing tricky. It's called attaching value to words and not talking like a motormouth. I am very careful about qualifying because that's what language is about.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: That's great. Then let me just ask a clarifying question, do you firmly believe that the majority of the 850 civilians were killed by Hamas?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: My view is, even if it were half, 400 is a huge number by any reckoning-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Why-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Okay, wait. You didn't-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I said even if-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Wait, wait, wait-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Because Benny, because Professor Morris, I don't know. I agree with Mouin Rabbani, I'm not sure if he concedes the 400. I'll say-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Why 400? Whoever thought up the number, 400? 800 of the 850 were slaughtered by Hamas. Maybe a couple of individuals were killed in Israeli action-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I don't know. Professor Morris-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: You're saying from day one, you believed this particular thing, and you clearly don't. You clearly don't believe this thing-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I said from day one day one-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: You said people died. That's not controversial-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Wait. Hold on, hold on. If I may-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: That's not controversial.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Mr. Bonnell, I attach value to words-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Yes, you've said that. If you value them, stop repeating them so much.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Mr. Bonnell, please slow down the speech and attempt to listen. When I was explicitly asked by Piers Morgan, I said there can be no question that Hamas committed atrocities-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Committed atrocities. I've heard this, yes.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: on October 7th. If you want me to pin down a number, I can't do that-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I didn't ask you to pin down a number. You can listen to what I'm-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You didn't ask me?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: No. My question is-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I'll ask a very precise-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Mr. Bonnell, I cannot speak to you because you're not-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Sorry, excuse me-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: It's a very easy question-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: If I understood your question correctly-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: My question is, do you think the majority of the people that were killed on October 7th, the civilians, were killed by Hamas, or are we subscribing to the idea that the IDF killed hundreds, four or 500 in the crossfire?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: No, but let me explain why that's a difficult question to answer. The total number of civilians killed was 800, 850. We know that Hamas is responsible, probably for the majority of those killings. We also know that there were killings by Islamic Jihad. We also know-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, we're Bunching together the Islamic Jihad and Hamas. That's splitting hairs now-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: But his question was specifically about-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, but he means the raiders. He means the raiders.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I'm speaking in opposition to the conspiracy theory that people like Do you prefer Norm or Professor Finkelstein? I don't know, how do you prefer to be addressed?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, it's not a conspiracy theory there because it's-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Well, the conspiracy theory is the idea that the IDF killed the majority of them.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: It's not a conspiracy theory-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: And there is also a theory that, as Norm pointed out on the show that he was on, that he thought that it was very strange that, given how reputable Israeli services are when it comes to sending ambulances, retrieving bodies, he thought it was very strange that that number was continually being adjusted-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Yeah, I did find it-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: And do you know why-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: So when you say that in combination with, “Well, I'm not sure how many were killed by Hamas and the IDF-“

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Do you know why the number went down? The number went down because the Israeli authorities were in possession of 200 corpses that were burned to a crisp that they assumed were Israelis who had been killed on October 7th. They later determined that these were in fact Palestinian fighters. Now, how does a Palestinian fighter get burned to a crisp?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, you're mixing two things. Some of the bodies, they weren't able to identify, and eventually they ruled that some of them were actually Arab marauders rather than Israeli victims. A few of them also of the Jews were burnt to a crisp and it took them time to work this out, and they came out initially with a slightly higher figure, 1,400 dead, and eventually reduced it to 1,200 dead Israelis-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: And the reason is that a proportion of Israeli civilians killed on October 7th I don't believe it was a majority. We don't know how many. Some were killed in the crossfire, some were killed by Israeli shellfire, helicopter fire and so on, and the majority were killed by Palestinians. And of that majority, we don't know Again, I understood your question is referring specifically to Hamas, which is why I tried to answer it that way. But if you meant generically Palestinians, yes. If you mean specifically Hamas, we don't have a clear breakdown of how many were-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: No, I don't mean specifically Hamas. But I just think when you use the word some, that's doing a lot of heavy lifting.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Who used some?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: That's fine. But some can mean anywhere from 1% to 49%-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Who used some?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: But we don't know.

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman
entries.text: So the numbers here in the details are interesting and important almost from a legal perspective, but if we zoom out, the moral perspective, are Palestinians from Gaza justified in violent resistance?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, Palestinians have the right to resistance. That right includes the right to armed resistance. At the same time, armed resistance is subject to the laws of war, and there are very clear regulations that separate legitimate acts of armed resistance from acts of armed resistance that are not legitimate-

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman
entries.text: The attacks of October 7th, where do they land for you?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: There's been almost exclusive focus on the attacks on civilian population centers and the killings of civilians on October 7th. What is much less discussed to the point of amnesia is that there were very extensive attacks on Israeli military and intelligence facilities on October 7th. I would make a very clear distinction between those two. And secondly, I'm not sure that I would characterize the efforts by Palestinians on October 7th to seize Israeli territory and Israeli population centers as in and of themselves illegitimate.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You mean attacking Israeli civilians is legitimate?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: No. That's not what I said.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I didn't understand what you said.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I think what you had on October 7th was an effort by Hamas to seize Israeli territory and population centers-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: And kill civilians.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: That's not what I said. What I said is, I would not describe the effort to seize Israeli territory as in and of itself illegitimate, as a separate issue from the killing of Israeli civilians in those cases where they had been deliberately targeted. That's very clearly illegitimate.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Whole families were slaughtered in kibbutzim, many of them left-wingers incidentally who helped Palestinians go to hospitals in Israel and so on, even drove Palestinian cancer patients to hospitals in Israeli-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Again, I'm making a distinction here-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: But you don't seem to be very condemnatory of what the Hamas did.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, I don't do selective condemnation-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I'm not talking about selective. I'm talking about-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I don't do selective outrage.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: specific condemnation of this specific assault on civilians. I would, for example, condemn Israeli assaults on civilians, deliberate assaults on civilians. I would condemn them, but you're not doing that with the Hamas.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: You know what the issue is?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: What?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I've been speaking in public now, I would say since the late-1980s and interviewed and so on. I have never on one occasion ever been asked to condemn any Israeli act. When I've been in group discussions, those supporting the Israeli action or perspective, I have never encountered an example where these individuals are asked to condemn what Israel is doing. The demand and obligation of condemnation is exclusively applied, in my personal experience over decades, is exclusively applied to Palestinians-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, this is [inaudible 02:25:04] Israel is condemned day and night on every television channel, and has been for the last-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I'm telling you about personal experience lasting decades-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You said quote-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Uh-oh. Oh, no.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I'm trying to quote what you just said. You said-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I shouldn't have said anything at any [inaudible 02:25:20]

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Professor Morris?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You just said, “I would condemn anytime Israel deliberately attacks civilians.” The problem, Professor Morris, is, over and over again, you claim in the face of overwhelming evidence that they didn't attack civilians-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: That's not true. I've said Israel has attacked civilians. In [inaudible 02:25:51] Israel attacked civilians, and I've written extensively about it. In Kfar Qasim, they killed civilians, and I've written that. So you're just admitting you're selecting As Steven says, you cherry-pick. You cherry-pick.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Okay, let's fast-forward. When you were an adult, what did you say about the 1982 Lebanon War?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: What did I say?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You don't remember? Okay, allow me.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Uh-oh.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Okay. So it happens that I had no interest in the Israel-Palestine conflict as a young man-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: This is true.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: until the 1982 Lebanon War.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Yep. He's lost the passage-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I'll find it.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Okay, real quick while he's searching for that, you bring up something that's really important that a lot of people don't draw a distinction between, in that there is just causes for war and there is just ways to act within a war, and these two things principally do have a distinction from one another.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Correct.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: However, while I appreciate the recognition of the distinction, the idea that the cause for war that Hamas was engaged in, if we look at their actions in war or the statements that they've made, it doesn't seem like it had to do with the territorial acquisition.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: No, no, no-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: By taking land back.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: No, the point I was making was, what was Hamas trying to achieve militarily on October 7th? And I was pointing out that the focus has been very much on Hamas attacks on civilians and atrocities and so on. And I'm not saying those things should be ignored. What I'm saying is that what's getting lost in the shuffle is that there were extensive attacks on military and intelligence facilities. And as far as the other aspects are concerned, because I think either you or Lex asked me about the legitimacy of these attacks, I said I'm unclear whether efforts by Hamas to seize Israeli population centers in and of themselves are illegitimate as opposed to actions that either deliberately targeted Israeli civilians or actions that should reasonably have been expected to result in the killings of Israeli civilians. Those strike me as, by definition, illegitimate, and I want to be very clear about that. I have-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Illegitimate means you condemn them?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Illegitimate means they are not legitimate. I have a problem-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Condemning your side, yes.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: No, not condemning my side. I have a problem with selective outrage and I have a problem with selective condemnation. And as I explained to you a few minutes ago, in my decades of appearing in public and being interviewed, I have never been asked to condemn an Israeli action, I've never been asked for a moral judgment on an Israeli action. Exclusive requests for condemnation has to do with what Palestinians [inaudible 02:29:01] And just as importantly, I'm sure if you watch BBC or CNN, when is the last time an Israeli spokesperson has been asked to condemn an Israeli act? I've never seen it.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I don't think we condemn the Arab side either though, right? I don't think there's any condemnation-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: No. But now that we're talking about Israeli victims, all of a sudden morality is central-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Well, I think the reason why it comes up is because there's no shortage of international condemnation for Israel. As Norm will point out a million times, that there are 50 billion UN resolutions, you've got Amnesty International, you've got multiple bodies of the UN, you've got now this case for the ICJ. So there's no question of if there's condemnation for Israel-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: But sorry, if I can interrupt you, in 1948, the entire world stood behind the establishment of a Jewish state, and the entire world-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, except Arab states and the Muslim states. Not the entire world.

entries.text: That's fine, but then the second thing is, or there's two distinctions I want to draw between. I think Benny would say this, I would say this. I'm sure, undoubtedly, there have been cases where IDF soldiers, for no good reason, have targeted and killed Palestinians that they should not have done, that would be prosecutable as war crimes as defined by the [inaudible 02:50:56]-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: And some have been prosecuted.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: And I'm absolutely sure-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: According to you and your book, practically none.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I'm sure that we would all agree for soldiers that that happens, but I think that it's important that when we talk about military strikes or we talk about things especially involving bombings or drone attacks, these are things that are signed off by multiple different layers of command, by multiple people involved in an operation, including intelligence gathering, including weaponeering, and they also have typically lawyers involved. When you make the claim that an IDF soldier shot a Palestinian, those three people, the three hostages that came up with white flags, that something horrible happened, I think that's a fair statement to make and I think a lot of criticism is deserved, but when you make the statement that four children were killed by a strike, the claim that you're making-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Deliberately, yeah.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: The claim that you're making is that multiple levels of the IDF signed off-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I have no idea what [inaudible 02:51:47]-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: That's great if you don't understand the process, then let me educate you.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You don't understand the process.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I do understand the process, I'm telling you. I'm trying to explain to you right now.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Really? You're in the IDF?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: No, it's basic-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You're studying the IDF.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: You can ask anybody that talks about-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Aside from Wikipedia, can you tell me what your knowledge of the IDF is?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: You can talk to people who work in the military-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: What's your knowledge of the IDF?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Your audience can look this up. Do you think that bombing and strikes are decided by one person in the field? Do you think one person-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Can I respond to that?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: [inaudible 02:52:10] on a drone strike-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: [inaudible 02:52:11] a pilot doesn't do it on his own.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Can I respond-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: [inaudible 02:52:14] have entire apparatuses that are designed to figure out how to strike and who to strike, so when you say that four children are targeted, you're saying that a whole apparatus that tries to murder-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You made my argument better than me-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Poor Palestinian children.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: You made my argument better than me.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Which is a ridiculous argument.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Oh, really? It's impossible at the command level, but you said that they couldn't have done it at the bottom if it weren't also at the top.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: You don't understand the strength of the claim that you're making. You're saying that from a top down level, that lawyers, multiple commanders, intelligence, all these people signed off-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Mr. Bonnell, do not tell me what I don't understand.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: On killing poor Palestinians, children.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: It's true, I don't spend my nights on Wikipedia. I read books. I admit that as a-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: That's a waste of time, by the way. You're wasting time [inaudible 02:52:55].

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I know, books are a waste of time. With all due regard, they're-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Well, according to you they are. The only thing you take from them are two or three quotes that you use to push people around.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I completely respect the fact And I'll say it on the air, as much as I find totally disgusting what's come of your politics, a lot of the books are excellent, and I'll even tell you because I'm not afraid of saying it, whenever I have to check on the basic fact, the equivalent of going to the Britannica, I go to your books. I know you got a lot of the facts right.

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman
entries.text: Benny Morris' books for the listener.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I would never say books are a waste of time and it's regrettable to you that you got strapped with a partner who thinks that all the wisdom-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: He didn't say they're a waste of time.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I'd like to respond to what you were saying. I think the question that we're trying to answer-

entries.text: I think you don't understand Israel, you know? Neither of you really understands Israel and how it works.

entries.text: I prefer the word murdered and raped rather than targeted. Targeted is too soft for what the Hamas did.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I'm okay with it.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I'm not talking about-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I'm talking about this now.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yeah, but I'm trying to answer his question. Historically, there is substantial evidence that Palestinians have targeted civilians, whether it's been incidental or systematic is a different discussion, I don't want to get into that now. For some reason, there seems to be a huge debate about whether any Israeli has ever sunk so low as to target a civilian. I don't-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, we've agreed. We've both said.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: We just agreed [inaudible 02:54:50].

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I just said that this has happened here and there. We've agreed on that. What we're saying is it's not policy, which is what you guys are implying, that they kill civilians deliberately.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: If I understand you correctly, you're basically making the claim that none of these attacks could have happened without going through an entire chain of commands.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: For strike cells that are involved in drone attacks or plane attacks or-

entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Yes [inaudible 02:55:13].

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: My understanding of the Israeli military, and you could perhaps You've served in it, you would know better, it's actually a fairly chaotic organization.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, that's not true, especially not the Air Force, extremely, extremely organized. The Air Force works in a very organized fashion, as he says, with lawyers, a chain of command, and ultimately the pilot drops the bomb where he is told to drop it.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Protective Edge, was that 200 strikes in like 60 seconds, I think, the opening of Protective Edge? The coordination between [inaudible 02:55:43]-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: You're talking about 2008.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: I think Protective Edge was 2014, but I'm just saying that the coordination in the military is pretty tight.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, my understanding of the Israeli military-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: It's very organized.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Is that it's quite chaotic and there's also a lot of testimonies from Israel, but be that as it may, I'm prepared to accept both of your contentions that it's a highly organized and disciplined force. Air Force under any scenario is going to be more organized than the other branches, and you're saying such a strike would've been inconceivable.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Well, I'm not necessarily saying inconceivable. I'm saying that that would've required murderous intent on so many different levels. I don't think good evidence has been presented to say that that's-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Your basic claim is that it would be fair to assume that such a strike could have only been carried out with multiple levels of authorization and signing off. Let's accept that for the sake of argument. We have now seen incident after incident after incident after incident where entire families are vaporized in single strikes-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Who is in the families? Who lives in the house inside-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Family members.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, next to the house in which these families are killed?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: We have seen incident-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Do you know that Hamas [inaudible 02:57:02] weren't in that house? Do you know that their ammunition dumps weren't in those houses?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Why do I have to prove a negative?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You are saying that they deliberately targeted families. If Israel wanted to kill civilians in Gaza, they could have killed 500,000 by now with the number of strikes they've done and the fact that they've only killed a certain small number [inaudible 02:57:22]-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: 30,000 is a small number?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Small number in proportion-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: You consider 30,000 a small number?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Small number in proportion over four months probably is an indication that-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: 12,000 children is only.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Is targeted and that there are Hamas targets in these places.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: 12,000 children is only, and if that's the case, why is it-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Did I use the word only?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Yeah, you said only. Professor Morris, here's a question for you, if we take every combat zone in the world for the past three years, every combat zone in the world-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: In Vietnam, the Americans killed 1 million people.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, the [inaudible 02:57:58] killed 40 million.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I was in the anti-war movement, so don't strap me-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: The Americans killed 1 million people in Vietnam.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Fine, and 30 million Russians were killed during World War II, so everything else is irrelevant.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: [inaudible 02:58:13].

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Professor Morris, here's a question, it's very perplexing. If you take every combat zone in the world for the past three years and you multiply the number of children killed by four, every combat zone in the world, you get Gaza. So when you say-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: What is that supposed to prove?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I'm going to tell you Just shut up

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Firstly, you're lying on Hamas numbers.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: No, I'm not lying [inaudible 02:58:44]-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Hamas numbers are not necessarily true.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: The numbers that everybody else I'm lying in the numbers [inaudible 02:58:47]-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Even if we take the numbers though, what does that prove?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Those are Hamas numbers, which may not be true. They could invent anything because you know that they are a mendacious organization.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I know mendacious, believe me-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You like the word mendacious?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Mendacious as in the Israeli Ministry of Foreign Affairs. So here's the thing, you say they could have killed 500,000, but they only killed, only, that's your words, they only killed 30,000.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You believe that they deliberately target civilians, they would've killed many, many more. The fact is that they don't deliberately target civilians.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Professor Morris, for [inaudible 02:59:24]-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: And you don't understand Israeli society.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I don't want to understand Israeli society.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You don't want the truth.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I don't want to. I D.dOn't want to get inside their heads.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: That's the problem.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: A good historian tries to get into the heads of-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: [inaudible 02:59:33].

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: There's a limit.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: The various protagonists.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: There's a limit.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: A good historian does.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: When 90% of Israelis think that Israel's using enough or too little force in Gaza, I don't want to get inside that head. 40% think that Israel is using insufficient force in Gaza. I don't want to get inside that head. I don't want to get inside the head of people who think they're using insufficient force against the population, half of which is children. I don't want to get inside that head, but here's the point, because your partner wants to know the point. You don't understand political constraints. One of your ministers said, “Let's drop an atomic bomb on Gaza.”

entries.text: You think he really meant that?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, no, no, it was said in a sort of a very questionable way. He didn't say they should drop an atomic bomb.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: He said it the day after the ICJ met.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: This minister is a messianic idiot, but he didn't say drop an atomic bomb [inaudible 03:00:43].

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: He said it [inaudible 03:00:44].

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: None other Israel's chief historian, the justifiably famed Benny Morris, thinks we should be dropping nuclear weapons on Iran.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Iran, its leaders for years have said, “We should destroy Israel.” Do you agree with that? They've said, “We should destroy Israel. Israel must be destroyed.” Is that correct? This is what the Iranian leaders have been saying since Khomeini.

entries.text: I would say Iranian leaders have sent mixed messages.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: But some of them have said, including Khamenei-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: If you don't know the evidence, why are you laughing?

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: It's funny because-

entries.text: Iran that supports Hezbollah and the Houthis and Hamas, maybe they want Israel destroyed.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Brace yourself to the extent that the Houthis are trying to stop the genocide in Gaza, I support-

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: [inaudible 03:01:37] ships. I know I selectively support international law when it agrees with you and then when it doesn't, you decide to throw international law to the wind.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: There's no genocide in Gaza.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: If you like [inaudible 03:01:46]-

entries.text: Hold on a second. Norm, Norm-

entries.text: Let me read what you said-

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman

The same question I asked on the Hamas attacks. Is there, from a legal, philosophical, moral perspective, is there genocide going on in Gaza today?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Is there a genocide going on in Gaza? Well, in several years we will have a definitive response to that question. What has happened thus far is that on the 29th of December, the Republic of South Africa instituted proceedings against Israel, pursuant to the 1948 convention on the prevention and punishment of the crime of genocide. South Africa basically accused Israel of perpetrating genocide in the Gaza Strip. On the 26th of January, the court issued its initial ruling. The court at this stage is not making a determination on whether Israel has or has not committed genocide. So, just as it has not found Israel guilty, it certainly also hasn't found Israel innocent. What the court had to do at this stage was take one of two decisions, either South Africa's case was the equivalent of a frivolous lawsuit and dismiss it and close the proceedings, or it had to determine that South Africa presented a plausible case that Israel was violating its obligations under the genocide convention and that it would on that basis hold a full hearing.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Now, a lot of people have looked at the court's ruling of the 26th of January and focused on the fact that the court did not order a ceasefire. I actually wasn't expecting it to order a ceasefire, and I wasn't surprised that it didn't because in the other cases that the court has considered, most prominently Bosnia and Myanmar, it also didn't order a ceasefire, and South Africa in requesting a ceasefire also didn't ask the court to render an opinion on the legitimacy, or lack thereof, of Israel's military operation. From my perspective, the key issue on the 26th of January was whether the court would simply dismiss the case or decide to proceed with it.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: And it decided to proceed [inaudible 03:04:35]-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: And I think that's enormously-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I thought that was beautifully [inaudible 03:04:39]-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: But you said they committed genocide. You already said they committed genocide. Israel is committing genocide.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: But if I can just-

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Allow me-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You used that word.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: That's correct. I don't run away from my words.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: So Norman, you did say Israel was committing genocide.

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman
entries.text: Norm, can you let Mouin finish?

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, the end of the story is you specifically asked whether I think Israel is committing genocide. I explained formally there is no finding and as you said, we won't know for a number of years and I think there's legitimate questions to be raised. In the Bosnia case, which I think all four of us would agree was clearly a case of genocide, the court determined-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You mean by the Serbs?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yes. In the Bosnia case, the court determined that of all the evidence placed before them only Srebrenica qualified as genocide and all the other atrocities committed did not qualify as genocide. International law is a developing organism. I don't know how the court is going to respond in this case, so I wouldn't take it as a foregone conclusion how the court is going to respond, but-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Norman has determined already.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I have too, because you're asking my personal opinion.

entries.speaker: Lex Fridman
entries.text: Personal opinion is [inaudible 03:05:50].

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: So as a matter of law, I want to state very clearly it has not been determined and won't be determined for several years. Based on my observations and the evidence before me, I would say it's indisputable that Israel is engaged in a genocidal assault against the Palestinian people in the Gaza Strip.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Which is a PLO line.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Get with the program, the PLO is long passed.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Okay, the Palestinian authority.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: As you were saying, genocide is not a body count. Genocide consists of two elements, the destruction of a people in whole or in part, so in other words, you can commit genocide by killing 30,000 people.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: [inaudible 03:06:39].

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, five probably is below threshold.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: There is a problem of numbers.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yes, but I think 30,000 crosses the threshold and not reaching 500,000 is probably irrelevant, and the second element is there has to be an intent. In other words-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: And you believe there's an intent?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yes. I think if there is any other plausible reason for why all these people are being murdered, it's not genocide. And as far as intent to [inaudible 03:07:06]-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: What about hiding behind a human shield? You don't think that's a reason for them being killed?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, let's get the intent part out of the way first. South Africa's-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: Forget South Africa, they don't [inaudible 03:07:16]-

entries.text: I'd like to finish.

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: No, they pro-Hamas.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: For some reason, you don't have a problem with people being pro-Israeli at the time of this, but if they support Palestinians' right to life or self-determination, they get demonized and de-legitimized as pro-Hamas?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: They supported an organization which murdered 1,200 people deliberately. That's my problem.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: But supporting a state that has murdered 30,000 [inaudible 03:07:45]-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: But they haven't because these are 30,000 are basically human shields to get by the Hamas, in which the Hamas wanted killed. They wanted them killed. Hamas wanted these people killed.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Sure, if I could just get-

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: You don't think they wanted them killed?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: No, I don't.

entries.text: They didn't provide them with shelters. They build tunnels for their fighters, but not one shelter for their own civilians.

entries.text: If I can get back to my point, you asked me about intent and the reason that I brought in the South African application is because it is actually exceptionally detailed on intent by quoting numerous-

entries.text: All sorts of idiotic ministers in Israel.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Well, yeah, including the prime minister, the defense minister, the chief of staff-

entries.text: The prime minister didn't say genocide [inaudible 03:08:27]-

entries.text: According to Asa Kasher, the philosopher of the IDF, he said that Netanyahu was vowing genocide. Now, he's an idiot?

entries.speaker: Benny Morris
entries.text: I didn't say he's an idiot, but he's passed it.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: So, the reason I raised the South African application is twofold. Hamas or no Hamas, it's exceptionally detailed on the question of intent. And secondly, when the International Court of Justice issues a ruling, individual justices have the right can give their own opinion. And I found the German one to be the most interesting on this specific question because he was basically saying that he didn't think South Africa presented a persuasive case, but he said their section on intent was so overpowering that he felt he was left with no choice but to vote with the majority. So, I think that answers the intent part of your question.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: So, for the ICJ case that South Africa has brought, I think there's a couple of things that need to be mentioned. One is, and I saw you two talk at length about this, the plausibility standard is incredibly low. The only thing we're looking for is a basic presentation of facts that make it conceivable, possible that-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Plausible.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Plausible, which legally, this is obviously below criminal conviction, below-

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Yes, of course. Think of it as an indictment.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: Sure, possibly, maybe even a lower level than even an indictment, so plausibility is an incredibly low standard, number one. Number two, if you actually go through and you read the complaint that South Africa filed, I would say that if you go through the quotes and you even follow through to the source of the quotes, the misrepresentation that South Africa does in their case about all of these horrendous quotes, in my opinion, borders on criminal.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: 16 ICJ judges disagree.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: That's fine if 16 ICJ judges disagree, but I'm going to give-

entries.text: They must be awfully incompetent.

entries.text: They could be.

entries.text: Even the American judge, she must have been awful incompetent if she was unable to see the misrepresentations that Mr. Bonnell based on his Wikipedia entry was able to find.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell
entries.text: So, this is based on the official ICJ report that was released. I'm not sure if you read the entire thing.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: I read every aspect.

entries.speaker: Steven Bonnell

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: Actually, brace yourself for this and Mouin could confirm it, Yaniv Kogan, an Israeli, and Jamie Stern-Weiner, a half Israeli, they checked every single quote in the Hebrew original and Yaniv Kogan, love the guy, he has terrifying powers of concentration, he checked every single quote. Is that correct, Mouin?

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: Mm-hmm.

entries.speaker: Norman Finkelstein
entries.text: And Jamie checked every single quote in the English, in the context, and where there were any contextual questions they told us.

entries.speaker: Mouin Rabbani
entries.text: I think they found one.
